front: What is the core interface of the Java Collections Framework for storing key-value pairs?
back: The Map<K,V> interface. It maps keys to values and cannot contain duplicate keys; each key can map to at most one value.

front: What are the three main general-purpose Map implementations and their key characteristics?
back: 1. HashMap: Unsorted, unordered map. Allows one null key and null values. 2. TreeMap: Sorted map according to the natural ordering of its keys or a Comparator. 3. LinkedHashMap: Ordered map, maintains insertion-order by default.

front: How does a ConcurrentHashMap achieve thread-safety and why is it often preferred over Hashtable?
back: It uses a mechanism called lock striping (segmenting the map and locking individual segments). It is preferred over the synchronized Hashtable because it allows concurrent read operations and higher-performance concurrent updates, providing better scalability.

front: What is the purpose of the equals() and hashCode() methods when using a custom object as a key in a HashMap?
back: The hashCode() method is used to determine the bucket location for the key-value pair. The equals() method is used to compare keys within the same bucket for equality. Both must be overridden correctly to ensure the map can store and retrieve entries properly.

front: What is the difference between the Collection views keySet(), values(), and entrySet() provided by a Map?
back: keySet(): Returns a Set view of the keys contained in the map. values(): Returns a Collection view of the values contained in the map. entrySet(): Returns a Set view of the key-value mappings (Map.Entry objects) contained in the map. This is often the most efficient way to iterate over a map.
